resources.string: No players found with that name.
No saved game found in the cloud.
Manage your public profile and backup your progress.
This will overwrite your previous cloud save with your current progress. Continue?
This will replace your current local progress with the data from the cloud. This cannot be undone. Continue?
Are you sure you want to sign out? Your local progress will be reset. If you haven't uploaded your save to the cloud, you will lose your current progress on this device.
Developed with Compose Multiplatform by Rafael Robles García
You need the following to unlock this item:
Are you sure you want to add 100k to all resources? This is for testing purposes.
Are you absolutely sure? This will delete all your progress, money and unlocked vegetables. This action cannot be undone.

You can spam clicks for small rewards, or wait 5 seconds until the tomato vibrates and glows intensely to get a massive 20x bonus! The Haptic Timing upgrade will help you feel exactly when the bonus is ready.
This broccoli moves! Tap it while it's zooming around to collect rewards. The 'Air Harvest' upgrade lets you collect rewards just by waving your hand near the sensor!
Bell peppers are erratic! They pause for a moment and then dash in a random direction. Wait for them to stop or try to catch them on the move!

Tap the garlic 10 times to make it explode into small cloves! Collect all the cloves to get a big bonus. The 'Shake to Harvest' upgrade lets you collect multiple cloves at once just by shaking your device!

The purple onion teleports! Every time it appears, it starts a quick rotation. If you catch it while it's spinning, you get a significant coin bonus! Buying more onions will increase your chances, but reduce the individual bonus slightly.

This is Zelda Tennis! The squash bounces back and forth. Click anywhere to slash with your sickle arm when the squash is near the bottom-left corner to hit it back. Don't miss, or you'll lose your momentum!

The apple moves in a circle, and you need to rotate your device like a compass to point at it. When the indicator turns green and you see 'COLLECTING!', rewards will be harvested automatically! No clicking required.
The tomato originates from the South American Andes, specifically in areas now known as Peru, Ecuador, and Chile.
Botanically, a tomato is a fruit because it develops from the ovary of a flower and contains seeds.
The first tomatoes to reach Europe were yellow, leading Italians to call them 'pomi d’oro' (golden apples).
Tomatoes are the main dietary source of lycopene, an antioxidant linked to many health benefits, including reduced risk of heart disease.
There are more than 10,000 varieties of tomatoes grown worldwide, ranging from small cherry tomatoes to giant beefsteak types.
Tomatoes have been successfully grown on the International Space Station to study plant growth in microgravity.
A fresh tomato is approximately 95% water. The remaining 5% consists mainly of carbohydrates and fiber.
Unlike many vegetables, cooking tomatoes increases the amount of lycopene your body can absorb.
The Guinness World Record for the heaviest tomato weighed 3.51 kg (7.7 lb) and was grown in the USA.
Each year, the town of Buñol in Spain celebrates a festival where 150,000 tomatoes are used as projectiles in a giant food fight.
Broccoli belongs to the Brassicaceae family, making it a close relative of cabbage, kale, and cauliflower.
The name 'broccoli' comes from the Italian word 'broccolo', which means the flowering crest of a cabbage.
One cup of broccoli contains as much vitamin C as an orange, helping to strengthen the immune system.
Broccoli produces natural sulfur compounds that act as a defense mechanism against insects and diseases.
Broccoli was a popular food in ancient Rome, where it was often served with various spices and oils.
It contains sulforaphane, a compound that may protect against certain types of cancer and improve heart health.
Steaming broccoli is the best way to cook it if you want to preserve its cholesterol-lowering properties.
The head of broccoli is actually composed of many tiny flower buds that haven't bloomed yet.
Over 90% of the broccoli grown in the United States comes from California due to its favorable climate.
Frozen broccoli can sometimes have more nutrients than fresh because it is frozen right after being harvested.
Bell peppers are the only member of the Capsicum family that doesn't produce capsaicin, making them sweet.
Most green bell peppers are just unripe red peppers. As they mature, they turn from green to yellow, orange, and then red.
Red peppers have 11 times more beta-carotene and 1.5 times more vitamin C than green ones.
They were first cultivated in Central and South America over 9,000 years ago.
Paprika is made by grinding dried bell peppers and other chili varieties, becoming a staple of Hungarian cuisine.
Pepper plants typically produce white flowers that eventually turn into the peppers we eat.
Because they contain more sugar, red peppers have a shorter shelf life than green peppers.
They can be eaten raw, roasted, stuffed, or fried, and are a key ingredient in 'mirepoix' or 'sofrito'.
Purple bell peppers exist! They are sweet like green ones but have a beautiful violet skin.
A high-quality pepper should feel heavy for its size, indicating thick, hydrated walls.
Garlic has been nicknamed 'The Stinking Rose' due to its strong aroma and beneficial properties.
Ancient Egyptians used garlic to treat everything from heart disease to parasites and even poor circulation.
The compound allicin only forms when garlic is crushed or chopped, initiating a chemical reaction.
Garlic was used during World War I as an antiseptic to prevent gangrene in soldiers' wounds.
The folklore that garlic repels vampires likely comes from its use as traditional medicine against blood-related diseases.
Eating parsley or drinking milk can help neutralize the sulfur compounds that cause garlic breath.
A single garlic bulb contains about 10 to 20 individual cloves, each protected by a papery skin.
Gilroy is known as the 'Garlic Capital of the World' and hosts an annual garlic festival that includes garlic ice cream.
Studies show that garlic can help reduce inflammation and may lower blood pressure in some people.
Garlic has been a staple in the diets of some of the longest-living populations in the world, such as in the Mediterranean.
The purple color comes from anthocyanins, powerful antioxidants also found in blueberries and red grapes.
Compared to white onions, red onions are often milder and sweeter, making them perfect for raw salads.
Onions release a gas called syn-propanethial-S-oxide when cut, which reacts with your eyes to form sulfuric acid.
Purple onion skins have been used for centuries as a natural dye for fabrics and Easter eggs.
Onions were worshipped in ancient Egypt; their spherical shape and concentric rings represented eternal life.
Purple onions are one of the richest sources of quercetin, a flavonoid that helps fight inflammation.
Regular consumption of red onions can help lower cholesterol and decrease the risk of heart disease.
Onions should be stored in a cool, dry place, but NOT next to potatoes, as they release moisture that rots the onions.
Red onions are the classic choice for pickling because the acid turns their color a vibrant neon pink.
Archaeological evidence suggests that onions have been a staple food for humans since the Bronze Age (around 3000 BC).
Zucchini (summer squash) grows incredibly fast, sometimes up to an inch per day under the right conditions!
Unlike winter squash, zucchini has thin, edible skin and is harvested while immature for the best flavor.
The ancestors of zucchini were first domesticated in Central and South America over 7,000 years ago.
The large bright yellow flowers of the zucchini plant are edible and are often stuffed or fried in many cultures.
Zucchini is 95% water and very low in calories, making it a favorite for weight-conscious diets.
The specific zucchini varieties we know today were developed in Italy in the late 19th century.
Zucchini is rich in potassium, vitamin C, and vitamin A, which are essential for heart and eye health.
Zucchini can be grilled, baked, sauteed, or even used in sweet breads and muffins.
While usually eaten whole, mature zucchini seeds are rich in healthy fats and protein.
Zucchini plants have separate male and female flowers and rely heavily on bees to transfer pollen between them.
Apples are members of the Rosaceae family, making them distant cousins of the rose and strawberry.
The modern apple's wild ancestor is Malus sieversii, which still grows in the mountains of Kazakhstan.
Apples are 25% air, which is why they float in water. This makes 'bobbing for apples' possible!
It would take you over 20 years to try every apple variety in the world if you ate one per day.
The tartness of an apple comes from malic acid. The word 'malic' comes from the Latin word for apple, 'malum'.
Apple seeds contain a small amount of cyanide. However, you'd need to chew and eat hundreds to get sick.
Apples release ethylene gas, which can help ripen other fruits like bananas or tomatoes if stored together.
The Bible never specifies that the fruit in the Eden was an apple; it was likely a pomegranate or a fig.
An apple a day really might keep the doctor away! Pectin in apples helps lower 'bad' cholesterol levels.
Apple trees can live for over 100 years, although their peak production years are usually between 10 and 50.
Aphids are tiny insects that suck the sap from plants, weakening them and spreading viral diseases.
Late blight (Phytophthora infestans) caused the Great Irish Famine, destroying potato crops in the 1840s.
Desert locusts can form swarms of billions of insects, capable of eating as much food as 35,000 people in one day.
These tiny arachnids thrive in hot, dry conditions and can quickly cover a plant in fine, suffocating webs.
Whiteflies secrete 'honeydew', which leads to the growth of black sooty mold on your crops' leaves.
A single tomato hornworm can defoliate an entire tomato plant in just a few days if left unchecked.
This fungal disease causes young seedlings to collapse and die at the soil line, often due to overwatering.
Many garden pests have evolved colors that blend perfectly with leaves, making them hard for predators to find.
Ladybugs and lacewings are a gardener's best friends, as they naturally hunt and eat harmful aphids.
Warmer winters allow more pests to survive until spring, leading to larger and more frequent pest outbreaks.
Farmers produce almost everything we eat, yet they make up less than 2% of the population in many developed nations.
Modern farmers must understand soil chemistry, moisture levels, and nutrient cycles to maintain healthy lands.
Many farmers now use GPS and satellite imagery to plant seeds and apply water with centimeter-level accuracy.
Approximately 90% of the world's farms are operated by families, producing about 80% of the world's food.
Crop rotation and cover cropping are ancient techniques farmers use to prevent soil depletion.
The average age of a farmer in many countries is around 58, highlighting the need for young people in agriculture.
Farmers often rent beehives during blooming seasons to ensure their crops are pollinated correctly.
A new generation of urban farmers is growing crops in vertical stacks inside buildings to save space.
Farming is one of the riskiest professions in the world because it depends entirely on unpredictable weather patterns.
Organic farmers must follow strict rules, including zero use of synthetic pesticides or fertilizers for several years.
Synthetic pesticides were first used widely after WWII to increase global food production.
Neem oil and soapy water are effective natural pesticides that are less harmful to the environment.
Certain pesticides, like neonicotinoids, have been linked to declining honeybee populations worldwide.
Chemicals can build up in the food chain, becoming more concentrated in animals that eat treated plants.
Overuse of chemicals causes pests to develop resistance, forcing farmers to use even stronger formulas.
IPM is a strategy that uses biological controls first and chemicals only as a last resort.
Rain can wash pesticides into rivers and lakes, affecting aquatic life and contaminating drinking water.
Herbicides are a type of pesticide used specifically to kill weeds that compete with crops for nutrients.
Farmers must wear specialized protective equipment when handling chemicals to prevent skin and lung irritation.
Governments set strict limits on the amount of pesticide residue allowed on fruits and vegetables sold in stores.
Genetically Modified Organisms have had their DNA altered in a laboratory to introduce desirable traits.
Humans have been 'genetically modifying' plants for thousands of years through selective breeding.
Golden Rice is a GMO designed to produce Vitamin A, aiming to prevent blindness in developing nations.
Scientists are developing crops that can survive with significantly less water to combat climate change.
BT corn produces its own natural toxin that kills specific harmful insects, reducing the need for sprayed pesticides.
The Flavr Savr tomato was the first genetically engineered food ever sold in stores (1994).
Genetic engineering can be used to make vegetables produce higher levels of vitamins and minerals.
GMOs are at the center of a debate regarding biodiversity, corporate control of seeds, and long-term health.
CRISPR is a new tool that allows scientists to edit plant genomes with much more precision than previous methods.
As the world population grows, genetic modification may be key to ensuring there is enough food for everyone.
Increases the frequency of double rewards. At level 5, rewards toggle (50%).
1% chance per level to get 1000% (10x) rewards on click.
Vibrates when the super reward is ready.
Double size, but requires 2 clicks per reward.
Collect rewards by passing your hand over the screen sensor.
Half movement cycle duration, but double rewards.
Double small garlics after explosion, +150% final reward.
Collect 5 small garlics at once by shaking your phone.
An extra onion appears, but bonus coins are reduced by 1.
A third onion appears, bonus coins are reduced further.
Onions spin for longer, widening the bonus window.
Each hit increases speed by 10% and adds +1 coin and +1 zucchini. Resets if you miss.
Double rotation speed, but you earn 5 coins per tick.
Unlock all fruits and vegetables in the garden.
Max out all global shop upgrades.
Get a 10-hit streak with the zucchini momentum modifier.
Perform 50 critical hits on the tomato.
Read all facts about bell peppers.
Read all facts about purple onions.
Unlock all information about genetic modification.
Do you want to unlock %1$s for %2$d coins?
Unlock all art pieces in the gallery.
Sign in to save your achievements and profile to the cloud.
Hi! I'm Clicky! Welcome to my garden.
The goal is simple: tap on fruits and vegetables to harvest them and earn money!
With that money, you can unlock crops and modifiers to become filthy rich!
You can also visit the Library to buy curious facts about what you grow.
And keep an eye out for Achievements! There's a lot to discover.
And if you connect your Google account you can share your profile and see your friends'.
That's all for now. Let's get growing!
Unlock powerful garden bonuses based on real-world weather for 5 hours after updating location.
Garlic and Onion: +1 coin and +1 fruit per click.
Broccoli and Apple: +1 coin and +1 fruit per click.
Tomato, Pepper and Zucchini: +1 coin and +1 fruit per click.
Every 5 clicks: +2 coins bonus on all vegetables.
10% discount on Library facts and Gallery art.
Tomato critical cycle reduced to 2 seconds.
Garlic reward multiplier increased to x2.0.
